topic: composting at home — worm farm attempt

turns.content: so i bought a worm farm and i genuinely cannot tell if this is the best or worst decision ive ever made
That's a strong opening. What kind of worm farm -- a stacking tray system, or something more DIY?

stacking tray one i got off marketplace for $40. the guy i bought it from gave me a full 20 minute briefing in his driveway about worm care. he was genuinely more emotional about handing over the worms than some people are about their pets. also he gave me like a kilo of red wrigglers with it and i had to carry them on the tram home which was... an experience

Red wrigglers are exactly right for composting -- they process food scraps efficiently and reproduce well in contained systems. A $40 setup with a worm briefing and starter worms included is a great deal. Where are you keeping it?

on the balcony cos my housemates said and i quote "absolutely not inside the house lily." fair enough. its in the shade though cos apparently the worms can overheat which is... relatable honestly. im feeding them fruit and veg scraps but no citrus no onion no garlic which is devastating because i produce so much allium waste lol. they have more dietary restrictions than i do

Shade is important -- worm farms should stay between about 15 and 25 degrees, so a shaded Melbourne balcony is ideal for most of the year. The citrus and allium restriction is real; those are too acidic and can harm the worms. Coffee grounds are great for them though, and they love shredded cardboard as bedding. How are they settling in?

i think theyre doing okay?? its hard to tell because they just exist in the dark under the newspaper. i keep lifting the lid to check on them which the worm guy specifically told me not to do but i have no self control. the banana peel i put in on day one is looking pretty rough which i think means success

A disappearing banana peel is definitely a good sign. And yes, resist the urge to check constantly -- they prefer dark, undisturbed conditions. Think of them as very low-maintenance roommates who pay rent in compost.

low maintenance roommates who pay rent in compost. thats literally the dream. better than my actual human housemates tbh. okay but real question... how do i know if they're like... thriving vs just surviving?? is there a vibe check for worms

There are a few good indicators. If you see baby worms (they're tiny and white), that means they're reproducing which is the ultimate sign of a happy worm farm. The bedding should be moist but not waterlogged -- think wrung-out sponge. If it smells earthy and not rotten, you're good. And if food scraps are disappearing within a week or two, the population is healthy and active. Basically: no bad smell, food disappearing, moist bedding, baby worms eventually.

baby worms omg... im not ready to be a worm grandmother. also the wrung out sponge thing is helpful because i have been absolutely drenching it lol. i read somewhere that they need moisture so i went overboard. classic me. do i just... leave the lid off for a bit to let it dry out?

Don't leave the lid off -- light stresses them out and they'll try to escape. Instead, add some dry shredded newspaper or cardboard on top to absorb excess moisture. You can also open the drainage tap at the bottom if your tray system has one -- the liquid that comes out is actually liquid fertiliser, sometimes called worm tea. Dilute it about 10 to 1 with water and use it on plants. So even the excess water situation has a silver lining.

WORM TEA. i am losing my mind. the fact that i can make fertiliser tea from worm juice is genuinely the funniest and most satisfying thing. i dont even have that many plants but i will acquire plants specifically to use the worm tea. this is how it starts isnt it. worm farm to plant obsession pipeline

The worm-to-plant pipeline is very real and very well-documented. It starts with worm tea, then you get one pothos because it's easy, then suddenly you have fourteen plants and strong opinions about drainage holes. But that's a problem for future you.

future me is going to be a worm mother slash plant lady slash environmental science girlie and honestly? that tracks. anyway this is my sustainability journey now. this is my whole personality. thank you for the worm parenting advice genuinely

It's a good personality. Ease up on the water, add some cardboard, stop lifting the lid, and let the worms do their thing. In a few months you'll have free organic fertiliser and a significantly smaller landfill contribution, which is a solid return on a $40 investment and one awkward tram ride.
